holdings.name: Vanguard FTSE All-World UCITS ETF (acc)
holdings.thesis: Single-ticker global equity core: ~3,700 stocks across 49 countries, market-cap-weighted. The portfolio's main growth engine.
risk: Drawdowns of 25-35% in global crashes are normal — sit through them, do not sell
~62% US weight means a US-led correction hits VWCE hard despite the 'world' label
Currency: ~60% USD exposure, EUR-strengthening years drag returns 5-10%
upside: Long-run real return ~5-7% p.a., nominal ~7-9% p.a. — the boring backbone
Auto-rebalances to winners (NVDA, Apple etc.) without manager risk
0.22% TER means almost no fee drag over 15+ years vs active funds

holdings.name: iShares Core MSCI World UCITS ETF (acc)
holdings.thesis: Legacy MSCI World position (~1,500 developed-market stocks). ~85% overlap with VWCE. Kept because rolling it into VWCE may trigger CGT.
risk: Almost full overlap with VWCE — diversification benefit ≈ 0
No emerging markets (~10% of world cap missed vs VWCE)
Two funds doing one job adds admin / Vorabpauschale paperwork
upside: Same engine as VWCE — won't underperform world equities
If sold at a small gain, freed cash buys VWCE simplification

holdings.name: BGF Next Generation Technology Fund D2 EUR (acc)
holdings.thesis: Active concentrated bet on next-gen tech (semis, AI infra, cyber, FinTech). Manager-picked, ~40-60 names, higher conviction than passive tech ETF.
risk: Sector-concentrated active fund: 45-55% drawdowns possible in tech/AI crash
1.80% TER drags ~1.6%/yr vs cheap alternative — needs alpha to justify
Manager risk: thesis-driven, can sit on losers longer than an index
upside: Tech-only with manager skill could outperform broad tech +2-4%/yr in a good cycle
Exposure to private-tech-adjacent themes not yet at large-cap weight in index
Compounds aggressively when narrative supports tech (AI, semi cycles)

holdings.name: Xtrackers MSCI World Information Technology UCITS ETF
holdings.thesis: Passive tech sleeve: MSCI World IT, ~150 stocks, ~70% US / 30% rest-of-world tech. Diversifies the BGF active bet at a 7x lower fee.
risk: Still single-sector — 40-50% drawdowns in tech crashes (similar to BGF, calmer)
Heavily Apple/Microsoft/Nvidia concentrated at the top
USD-denominated effectively → EUR/USD swings amplify returns
upside: Tech beta at 0.25% TER — lets BGF keep its edge while you ride the sector cheaply
Long-run tech has outpaced world equity +3-5%/yr (path-dependent, not guaranteed)
Easy to scale up/down — liquid ETF, no Ausgabeaufschlag

holdings.name: onemarkets J.P. Morgan Emerging Countries Fund M EUR (acc)
holdings.thesis: Active EM mandate wrapped by HVB/Structured Invest, run on a JPMorgan emerging-markets sleeve. Bet on manager + EM convergence vs passing through EIMI/JREM.
risk: ~2.0% TER is heavy — manager needs ~1.7%/yr alpha vs MSCI EM net to beat JREM (0.35%)
Onemarkets wrapper adds counterparty / structure layer (Structured Invest GmbH)
EM volatility + FX + political risk: 35-45% drawdowns plausible
upside: Active EM has historically been one of the few sleeves where managers do beat benchmark — if this one is in that camp, fee earns out
Asset breakdown more sensible vs cap-weighted EM (less China-tech concentration)
EM mean-reversion play: developed-market valuations stretched; EM cheap on most metrics
